case_name: NKX v Barts Health NHS Trust [2020] EWHC 828 (QB) (08 April 2020)

summary.brief: Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: The Claimant's case that his mother was given no or no sufficient warning that she should have continuous fetal monitoring (CFM) when she was in labour
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Central to the case on breach of duty is whether the Defendant's midwifery staff took reasonable steps to inform the Claimant's mother
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: I accept the expert evidence of Ms Cro, reporting on behalf of the Claimant, that midwifery staff ought to have confirmed as much by carrying out a vaginal examination
evidence_index.speaker: Expert testimony

evidence_index.text: I consider that there was a breach of duty on the part of the Defendant during the night of the 23rd and 24th May.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: I consider that Consultant Midwife Falvey-Browne appropriately balanced the need to make it clear that IA was not recommended
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: I have concluded that I have confidence in all of the auscultations performed until 01.00 hours.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: It is recorded in the notes that there were IAs carried out by Midwife Amoako at 23.20, 23.40 and 24.00 hours and that they were all normal.
evidence_index.speaker: Medical record

evidence_index.text: Dr Thomas, Paediatric Neurologist, confirms that the Claimant is 'permanently handicapped by the effects of an evolving, but predominantly dystonic, four-limb cerebral palsy.'
evidence_index.speaker: Expert testimony

evidence_index.text: It follows that there must be judgment for the Claimant on the basis that but for the breaches of duty that occurred at the maternity unit
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: I prefer to adopt the extended Myers model. I do so because I consider that although there was a persistent bradycardia after 01.14 hours there was some oxygenation
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient is a candidate for VBAC, previously had a caesarean section. Expressed desire for a water birth and was interested in birthing center options.

requestables.name: Midwifery Consultation
requestables.description: Discussion of birth plan and monitoring options

reveal.result_summary: Patient informed of VBAC risks, monitoring options discussed
reveal.decision_impact: Should guide choice of continuous fetal monitoring

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents in labor at 41 weeks gestation

key_events.event: Patient expresses desire for water birth and IA

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in labor, considering monitoring options
decision_points.prompt: What monitoring method should be recommended for a VBAC candidate?

options.clinical_reasoning: CFM provides early detection of fetal distress and uterine rupture
IA is less effective in detecting early signs of uterine rupture

actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Patient's preference for water birth and IA

expected_action_court.description: Continuous fetal monitoring should have been recommended
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence supports CFM as standard for VBAC

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: IA does not provide adequate monitoring for VBAC risks
explanation.what_should_have_happened: CFM should have been used to detect potential complications

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in labour with continuous pain
decision_points.prompt: What should be done in response to continuous pain during labour?

options.clinical_reasoning: Continuous pain is a sign of possible uterine rupture
Pain was not recognized as a sign of rupture

actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Pain was not identified as a critical sign

expected_action_court.description: Obstetric assistance should have been called
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence indicated need for immediate intervention

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to recognize continuous pain as a sign of uterine rupture

patient_outcome.description: Claimant sustained severe brain damage due to hypoxic-ischaemic insult
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Dystonic cerebral palsy, severe cognitive impairment

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to provide continuous monitoring
Causation established - earlier intervention could have prevented severe damage

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to provide continuous fetal monitoring
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Continuous monitoring would have detected fetal distress earlier

factual_timeline.event: Patient arrives at hospital in labor

allegations.allegation: Failure to recommend continuous fetal monitoring
Failure to provide continuous fetal monitoring
